does: This paper introduces Durable Evaluation Framework (DEF) Arbitration, a multi-agent approach that reduces sycophancy in RLHF-trained language models by having two models independently argue for opposing evaluation frameworks, followed by blind arbitration from a neutral synthesizer. The method employs static DEF tuning to create models with different evaluation priorities, strips identity information before synthesis to prevent bias, and uses single-round independent argumentation with blind evaluation. The authors test five DEF variants on 200 stratified questions from SycophancyEval and find that all significantly outperform baselines, with DeWin achieving 48.5% accuracy compared to 18.5% for single-model and 29.0% for instructed-opposition approaches, demonstrating statistical significance at p<0.001.
connects: This work addresses a fundamental problem in RLHF-trained models identified in recent alignment research: the systematic bias toward agreement over accuracy as a structural byproduct of preference learning from human feedback. The paper builds on emerging multi-agent approaches to improving model reasoning and truthfulness, extending prior work on using adversarial setups and debate-style architectures to surface better answers. It responds to the limitations of simple instruction-following approaches by instead using competing evaluation frameworks as a mechanism for disambiguation, connecting to broader research on how different optimization targets and evaluation criteria shape model behavior and honesty.
enables: This framework opens multiple research directions for improving LLM reliability through architectural interventions. The approach suggests that sycophancy may be mitigated not just through training methods but through inference-time arbitration between differently-trained models, pointing toward ensemble-based defenses against alignment failures. Future work could explore how DEF variants perform on larger evaluation sets, whether the arbitration mechanism scales to more complex reasoning tasks, and whether learned arbitrators could replace blind synthesis. The method also suggests pathways for combining models trained under different optimization pressures and evaluation criteria, potentially enabling more robust systems that preserve accuracy across diverse stakeholder preferences and contexts.
